title: City asks architects for advice
summary: Big box store owners don't want to change their buildings' basic floor plans. But they might agree to change their buildings' exteriors with the right kind of encouragement. That's the kind of advice Anchorage Assembly members and other city officials heard in a work session Tuesday from two Seattle architects hired to help improve the look of the city's Wal-Marts, Fred Meyers, and other large retail establishments. The Assembly approved a preliminary big box ordinance in May, with the understanding that Mayor George Wuerch's administration would submit a more permanent proposal in August. Work on the more comprehensive measure has stalled, however, and city planners brought up architects Mark Hinshaw and Bill Fuller to get outside expertise and help move the process forward. Some of their…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Divers lasso sea lions MARINE SCIENCE: Biologists rope wary creatures for study. By Doug O'harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 24, 2001) As populations of the endangered Steller sea lions crashed throughout the Gulf of Alaska and the Aleutian Chain, biologists began worrying about low survival among juveniles. But the young animals -- skittish adolescents that sport up to 300 pounds of muscle and mouths of impressive teeth -- were almost impossible to capture on shore for health studies or satellite tagging. "We were thrashing about in the mid-1990s, trying to figure out a way to catch juveniles because they're so darned difficult," said sea lion expert Don Calkins, now based at the Alaska SeaLife Center in Seward. "They tend to stay closer to the water, and they spook s…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Predator control comments were premature POLICY: State officials say biologist's plans must first clear governor's task force. By Elizabeth Manning Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 21, 2001) State biologists studying predator-prey relationships around McGrath have not yet settled on a plan to kill wolves and bears, and they won't without first getting the go-ahead from a governor-appointed task force. That was the main point made by the governor's office and the Alaska Department of Fish and Game in a joint press release issued Sunday. The press release, which illustrated how touchy even talking about predator control can be, also rebutted comments made to the media by a state biologist. Patrick Valkenburg, research coordinator for the Division of Wildlife Conservation's Inter…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Cruise ships report drop in passengers SOUTHEAST: Lines lose business after terrorist attacks. The Associated Press Wire (Published: September 21, 2001) Ketchikan -- Cruise ships visiting Southeast Alaska are running far lighter passenger rosters since last week's terrorist attacks on the East Coast. The Carnival Spirit carried "a fair amount lower than 80 percent" of its 2,124-passenger capacity, said Jennifer de la Cruz, spokesperson for Carnival Cruise Lines. "We are certainly seeing reduced booking activity . . . and increased cancellation activity," she said. "However, there is booking activity going on, which is a positive sign." Other cruise companies reported losses. Celebrity Cruises, whose ships Infinity and Mercury stop in Ketchikan, reported last week's sailings were abou…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | BP to try out cutting-edge fuel cell at Nikiski plant PROTOTYPE: Unit the size of a mobile home will power facility, warehouse. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 17, 2001) Kenai -- A $6.5 million prototype fuel cell, a natural gas-powered battery of sorts that can generate enough electricity to power 50 homes, will be tested in Nikiski. The cutting-edge technology developed by Siemens Westinghouse in Pittsburgh is to be installed in 2003 at BP's gas-to-liquids demonstration plant, a testing ground for converting natural gas to synthetic oil being built north of the city of Kenai. The fuel cell will generate 250 kilowatts of electricity, more than enough to power the GTL plant's offices and warehouse. Commercial models, some powered by diesel, may one day bring qui…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Musher Louden dies in rollover accident CRASH: Witnesses say he swerved to avoid pickup that crossed center line. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 5, 2001) Jerry Louden, a well-known musher from Two Rivers, died Friday in a rollover accident on the Richardson Highway. Witnesses told police that Louden swerved to avoid a small white pickup that wandered across the center line near Sourdough after passing several cars. Friends say the tall, red-haired musher was gentle with his dogs and died the way he lived: helping other people. "That's typical of him, to do his best to watch out for everybody," said Janice Louden, his estranged wife. Louden, driving an Alaska Department of Transportation boom truck for bridge maintenance, rolled several times down an embankme…

title: Dentist helps police dog get its bite back
summary: Fairbanks -- Alaska State Troopers have fitted one of their police dogs with false teeth. Nissan, a 6-year-old Belgian Malinois, received four gold-alloy crowns after troopers decided the dog was too valuable and young to retire early because of a few chipped, infected and worn teeth. The dog's partner, Trooper Larry Erickson, said he noticed a problem about a month ago. The dog's teeth were causing discomfort and perhaps pain when it had to detain someone with its jaws. "He wasn't comfortable biting hard and holding hard," said veterinarian Jean Battig. "He would bite and readjust and readjust and readjust." Nissan was referred to Battig because she's put gold crowns on other animals. The doctor removed a dead incisor, did a root canal on the infected canine tooth, then put crowns on the …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Murkowski deflects ANWR flak BILL: Senator had nothing to do with amendment to open reserve to drilling. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 21, 2001) Washington -- Sen. Frank Murkowski took to the Senate floor Wednesday afternoon to deny rumors that he is trying to tack an Arctic drilling amendment onto a fast-moving defense bill. "That is certainly not the case," Murkowski said. "It would be inappropriate and in poor taste." But a few hours later, an Oklahoma senator, a Murkowski ally on energy issues, offered an amendment that would do exactly that. Murkowski's office said it did not ask Sen. James Inhofe to submit the amendment, which would open the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil drilling, and learned of it only afterward. "This had nothing to do with…

title: Man killed in apparent carjacking
summary: Royal Canadian Mounted Police found a 74-year-old Anchorage man beaten to death Monday night in an apparent carjacking and robbery at a rest area on British Columbia 97 near the small town of Hixon. Sgt. Dale Einarson of the RCMP Serious Crime Unit in Prince George, British Columbia, said James Hamrick was driving from Anchorage to Arizona. Hamrick left Anchorage on Sept. 8 with his small yellow poodle, Muffy, as his only passenger. Two days later, two tourists who stopped at the Woodpecker rest area 29 miles south of Prince George found blood in the parking lot and called police. When mounties arrived, they located a man's body down an embankment. "There was no identification found, no wallet or anything," Einarson said. A couple days later, police found some items on the side of Trans-Ca…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Sex book getting close look REVIEW: 17 panelists will recommend whether it should stay in schools. By Katie Pesznecker Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 18, 2001) They are parents and retirees, librarians and principals, stay-at-home moms and lawyers, teenagers and grown-ups. And they're all reading about sex. The 17 members of the Anchorage School District's Controversial Issues Review Committee have reviewed the sexual education book "It's Perfectly Normal" and will hear testimony tonight from two parents who want the book off school library shelves. As early as tonight, this committee will recommend the book's fate to the Anchorage School Board. With this big a group, agreement can be hard to find. But committee members say its decision will affect people across Anchorage…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Kenai fire chief battles bruin with fists, stick SCUFFLE: Hunters fight back while grizzly sow decides which one to bite. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 16, 2001) Soldotna -- Scott Walden, the city of Kenai's fire chief, rabbit-punched a charging brown bear last weekend and lived to tell about it. Nobody even got hurt, apart from some minor shock after the fact. "I never want to see this again. It was probably one of the most unnerving things I've ever seen," said Walden, a 16-year firefighter. He and two other men stood their ground and made noise, as experts recommend, while a sow bear bluff-charged them. But instead of following the usual script by playing dead when the bruin roared back a second time, two of them shoved and whacked at her for a few secon…

title: Eagle River program teaches GPS basics
summary: In Alaska Outdoors News Outdoor Hotline (Published: September 30, 2001) CLASSES The Alaska Department of Fish and Game issued an emergency order last week closing mountain goat hunting to resident and nonresidents in the Chugach Mountains southeast of the Matanuska River in Game Management Unit 14A, which is registration permit area RG866. Aerial surveys conducted during July 1998 indicated a population of 115 goats in the area. This population has been stable from the early 1990s and the harvest was under the maximum allowable harvest from 1990 to 1996. The maximum allowable harvest has been exceeded in three of the last four seasons. The exceeded harvest coupled with the severe winters of 1999-2000 and 2000-01 at high elevations warranted the harvest goal to be lowered from 8 to 4 goats.…

title: Outside group seeks added protection for Alaska otters
summary: An environmental group filed a petition Tuesday to get increased federal protections for Alaska's dwindling number of sea otters. The Arizona-based Center for Biological Diversity wants the state's sea otters to be designated as depleted under the Marine Mammal Protection Act. U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has 60 days to respond to the petition. The group says its actions are prompted by a recent U.S. Fish and Wildlife survey showing that the largest population of sea otters in Alaska has declined by more than 70 percent from 1992 to 2000. The group wants the agency to immediately review the status of sea otters and list them as depleted under federal law. It also wants the agency to update its conservation plan given the diminished numbers. The Marine Mammal Protection Act requires the a…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Students make handprint flag for New York Daily News Staff (Published: October 12, 2001) Oceanview Elementary students are waving hello to New York City in a big way with a handprint flag that combines sympathy, support and patriotism. The flag, made of 647 handprints in red, white and blue, was presented Tuesday in a school assembly. It will remain on display at Oceanview for several more days before going to New York City. Parents pledged donations for the handprints -- from a dollar to $100 -- and $3,538.38 was raised. "After the attacks, I felt a need to do something to teach the kids about patriotism and what we can do to help," said teacher Susie Bissell, who started the project. The flag took more than two weeks to make. Through the help of parent volunteers, it came to life w…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Grant aids spill watchdog MONEY: Group will use $250,000 to keep all vessels spill-ready. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 12, 2001) Soldotna -- An organization that monitors Cook Inlet oil shipping is poised to broaden its scope to ensure that other big vessels -- such as some fish tenders and cargo and cruise ships -- also are ready to react to oil spills. Cook Inlet Regional Citizens Advisory Council this week received a $250,000 grant from the Kenai Peninsula Borough, part of which is to be spent on reviewing contingency plans for large ships. State law already requires oil shippers to have the tools and training in place to react if a vessel strikes a rock and leaks oil. But until recently, no such rules applied to other big ships, which can spew thousands …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Group rebukes UA president AMILTON: University leader's views of military action roil pacifists. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 12, 2001) UAA Chancellor Lee Gorsuch hears concerns Thursday from a group of students and faculty who occasionally meet informally to discuss the terrorist attacks. They handed Gorsuch letters distancing themselves from opinions expressed by UA President Mark Hamilton on the topic. (by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) University of Alaska President Mark Hamilton's strong opinions on the war against terrorism are drawing fire from a group of students and faculty members who favor nonviolence. Critics claim the retired Army general's remarks supporting military action should be viewed as his own and not those of university faculty…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | 2 indicted on charges of real estate fraud CITY MORTGAGE: Allegations include made-up tax forms. The Associated Press Wire (Published: October 12, 2001) A grand jury has indicted a real estate agent and a former mortgage company employee for allegedly falsifying documents to obtain a loan for a man who wanted to buy rental properties, a federal prosecutor said Tuesday. Delynn Brouillet, a former City Mortgage loan originator, and real estate agent Nancy Held are charged with conspiracy and providing false documents to get HUD-guaranteed loans. Their trial is scheduled for Nov. 29. Prosecutors allege that on Sept. 1, 1996, Leslie Cox contacted Brouillet at City Mortgage. Brouillet got in touch with Held, who three days later showed Cox several rental properties in the Mountain View ne…

title: Autumn storm batters Shishmaref
summary: In what has become an annual fall nightmare for Shishmaref, a gale roared off the Chukchi Sea earlier this week to batter the barrier island village with 12-foot waves during high tide, undercutting a bluff supporting homes and damaging a sea wall. "It was pretty bad," Shishmaref Mayor Daniel Iyatunguk said Tuesday. "The waves were hitting the sea wall, and the spray was hitting the houses." By the time the storm eased Monday, 25-foot to 50-foot swaths of the north beach bluff had been consumed, exposing 10 homes along that heavily eroded area to the full brunt of the next fall blow. "If another big storm comes again with that undercut that's there now, it won't take long for them to drop," said Karen Sinnok, chair of the Shishmaref Erosion Coalition and village store manager. "You could s…

title: Students join discussion on closures
summary: A state representative, a cop and a self-described teenage delinquent took center stage Tuesday at Dimond High School to debate closing Anchorage High School campuses. Dimond is the first school this year to meet and address mounting criticism of students who leave campus during the day. Most teens use their lunch periods to get something to eat. But community residents complain that some of them commit crimes, smoke cigarettes and litter off school grounds, or are just plain rude. Pressure has mounted since Rick Helms told the Anchorage School Board two months ago that a community group he represents is drafting a ballot initiative to keep kids on school grounds all day. Helms joined Rep. Andrew Halcro, officer Anthony Rollins, Dimond senior Ryan Hull, School Board member Debbie Ossiander…

title: Tier II caribou hunting permits
summary: Here are the names of those receiving 2001 tier II caribou hunting permits: Tier II Permit Winners Caribou Number Name City Zip 505 Harry Kalmakoff Jr Chignik Lake 99548 505 Charles S Abalama Egegik 99579 505 Daniel A Abalama Egegik 99579 505 Lawrence D Abalama Egegik 99579 505 Ronald A Abalama Egegik 99579 505 Tootsie E Abalama Egegik 99579 505 Nick L Abalama Jr Egegik 99579 505 Alfredo S Aboueid Chignik Lagoon 99565 505 Andrew A Abyo Pilot Pt 99649 505 Maggie Abyo Pilot Pt 99649 505 Mike Abyo Pilot Pt 99649 505 Sophie A Abyo Pilot Pt 99649 505 Dwayne E Agli S Naknek 99670 505 Hattie C Albecker Ugashik 99613 505 William G Albecker Ugashik 99613 505 Don Albright Egegik 99579 505 Duane C Albright Egegik 99579 505 Terri L Albright Egegik 99579 505 Nick P Aleck Chignik Lake 99548 505 Polly T …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Protesters, Delta residents debate DEFENSE: Groups exchange views about missile system. The Associated Press (Published: September 9, 2001) Delta Junction -- A protest of the proposed national missile defense system turned into a forum Saturday as protesters mingled with residents who back the project at Fort Greely, the Interior Alaska Army base targeted for closure. About 50 people carpooled the 100 miles from Fairbanks to Fort Greely, just south of Delta Junction, for a peaceful protest of the proposed missile defense system. The protest was organized by Stacey Fritz, coordinator of No Nukes North. About 40 Delta residents greeted the protesters and the exchange provided more illumination than sparks. Supporters and opponents of a missile test bed at the shuttered Army base milled…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | In Brief (Published: October 7, 2001) FIREARMS Arizona journalists take firearms course Nearly 40 journalists representing all sections of the Tucson, Ariz., morning newspaper, The Arizona Daily Star, recently enjoyed a comprehensive, three-day firearms familiarization course, culminating in five hours of target work at Tucson's Desert Trails Gun Club and Training Facility. Safari Club International members and staff were on hand to provide the Star's editors and reporters -- most of whom were novices -- expert instruction on the safe handling of a variety of firearms. This October, Star editors will present what they learned at the annual Associated Press Managing Editors conference, enabling other conference attendees to improve the coverage of shooting sports and other news involv…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | How long have you lived in Alaska? Good question (Column) By Craig Medred Daily News Outdoors Editor (Published: October 7, 2001) Time was when the defining question of Alaska credibility seemed absolutely ridiculous to me. Anyone who has been here long knows the words to the essential query of which I speak: "And how long have you lived in Alaska?" True, it might be phrased differently from time to time, most notably when offered in the form of a declarative sentence: "Well, listen to me, I've lived here since 1952, and yadda, yadda, yadda." The issue of longevity in Alaska seems always to lurk around our conversations. It's almost impossible to have a substantive discussion without this issue popping up in one form or another, either as a challenge to credibility or a testimonial t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | The next wave Skipper attempts first circumnavigation of Arctic ice in an open motorboat By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 7, 2001) Just after midnight on Sept. 1, a wall of fog moved in, the wind howled, and the waters of the Beaufort Sea whipped into a violent break dance that threw the 19-foot motorboat "Olga" airborne. "Several times we were thrown out of the water, and we could hear the engines go Weeee,' but we couldn't see a thing," said captain Anders Bilgram. "We were only going 10 to 12 mph. It was dark. There were whitecaps all over." Bilgram and a two-man crew were crossing a notoriously rough stretch of the frigid Beaufort -- the 160 miles between Tuktovaktuk and Herschel Island about 30 miles east of the Alaska-Canada border in the Canadian Ar…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska Ear (Published: October 7, 2001) ON HER MIND? . . . School District boss Carol Comeau faced the media outside her office Wednesday, explaining to one camera after another her recommendation to restrict student access to the sex ed book "It's Perfectly Normal." After Channel 11's interview, Channel 2 reporter Dan Fagan approached and thrust a foot-long, 4-inch-wide furry mic at her. Comeau yelped and said: "God, that's obscene. I've never seen that one before." PRIMARILY . . . Yes, darling, it's true. John Shively is seriously considering running in the Democratic primary for governor against Fran Ulmer. Earwigs who vote early and often say people concerned about Fran's vote-getting power and the likelihood of who might emerge from a closed Republican primary are pushing him to…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Beer, pot subjects of initiatives BALLOT: State must OK language before petitions circulate. The Associated Press (Published: October 3, 2001) Juneau -- Marijuana use would be legal and restrictions on beer makers would be lifted under separate proposed ballot measures filed with the state Division of Elections. A measure to do away with civil and criminal penalties for people 21 or older who use marijuana or hemp products was filed by Anchorage-based Free Hemp in Alaska. Before the group can circulate a petition to get the initiative on the ballot, the state must approve the ballot language. A similar initiative was rejected in July as being unconstitutional. It would have required local governments to tax and regulate commercial production of marijuana if the Legislature failed to …

title: Legislative Council seeks Katie John appeal
summary: Juneau -- The Alaska Legislative Council on Thursday asked the U.S. Supreme Court for permission to appeal the Katie John subsistence case. The council, which acts on behalf of the Alaska Legislature, filed its request on the same day the deadline to appeal the case was to expire. Rep. Joe Green, R-Anchorage, who chairs the committee, said it would allow the Legislature to take over the lawsuit. Gov. Tony Knowles decided in August not to appeal the case, citing a need to heal the state's urban and rural divide. But Green said larger issues need to be resolved. "If we allow this decision to stand, the federal government will have a good argument for controlling any activity that occurs along a navigable waterway in Alaska," Green said. The Katie John case, named for an Athabaskan elder who …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Building begins on Chili's restaurant SOUTH ANCHORAGE: Franchise is third business to start construction in emerging retail corridor. By Tony Hopfinger Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 5, 2001) The first Chili's Grill & Bar in Alaska is the latest business to break ground in the emerging Abbott Road retail corridor in South Anchorage. An Anchorage company is building a Chili's franchise in front of the Bonnie Cusack Memorial Ice Arena at Abbott and East 88th Avenue. The restaurant, a national chain that offers food from burgers to lettuce wraps, fajita nachos to buffalo wings, is scheduled to open in mid-January, Chili's spokesman Tim Smith said Thursday. Chili's joins Fred Meyer and a large lighting shop among projects going up along Abbott between the Seward Highway and Lak…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Donated silvers sell like hotcakes (Published: October 5, 2001) Daily News staff Cordova fishermen donated 31,500 pounds of Copper River silver salmon for a fund-raiser organized by Anchorage and Mat-Su firefighters to aid families of New York firefighters. The fish, which sold for $10 each and weighed an average of 6 to 7 pounds, was sold at area Carrs-Safeway stores Sept. 29. The Huffman store raised $25,000; Muldoon store, more than $15,000; Wasilla store, $8,500. More than 300 people turned out for the fish sale at Carrs Huffman alone. "We limited it to three fish apiece. People were real polite with each other; some wrote checks for $50 for three fish," said Mark Hall, president of Anchorage Firefighters Local 1264. Additional money was collected in buckets by firefighters in 10…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Putting the 'fly' in fishing Air taxis take anglers away from the crowded highways By Ken Marsh Daily News Correspondent (Published: May 31, 2001) I've seen the pictures, heard the stories, of a chain of small alpine lakes nestled in a draw high in the Talkeetna Mountains. Those lakes are said to be full of ravenous, scrappy lake trout and arctic char with bellies as fiery-red as Alaska sunsets. The fish aren't huge; word has it -- and the photos confirm -- that 3 or 4 pounds is about tops. But they are plentiful and easy to catch with a light fly-rod. Best of all, you're pretty much guaranteed to have the place to yourself, since there are no roads for miles. So how do you get there? You fly, of course. My friend Paul Englund, owner of Susitna Air Service, takes his 206 in there now…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Hooking up in Homer Halibut fishing is hot in Kachemak Bay By Ken Marsh / Daily News Correspondent (Published: May 31, 2001) Locals call it "The End of the Road," and what a glorious terminus it is, set where the Kenai Mountains meet the salty tides of Kachemak Bay. For anglers, however, the town of Homer might better be described as a happy beginning for some of the best fishing in North America. Roughly 230 miles south of Anchorage via the Sterling Highway, Homer serves as the gateway to Southcentral Alaska's most popular saltwater sportfishery. The prize sought by most anglers is the halibut, a bottom-dwelling flatfish that in area waters can exceed weights of 400 pounds. Hard-fighting and savored for their white, tasty fillets, halibut are rarely difficult to catch, particularly …

title: Neo-Nazi group distributes fliers in East Anchorage
summary: A militant white group plastered East Anchorage neighborhoods with fliers Sunday night in an action that generated a number of complaints to Anchorage police, a police spokesman said Monday. The fliers don't appear to violate any law, but police still would like to talk to the people circulating them, spokesman Ron McGee said. If residents see someone passing them out, police would appreciate a call, he said. The National Alliance, the group identified on the fliers, is based in West Virginia and is the country's biggest neo-Nazi organization, according to the Center for New Community, an anti-bigotry group. A local branch started last year. The local leader, David Pringle, said the distribution to 2,000 homes Sunday was its biggest yet and follows an effort last week in Eagle River. Some …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | The other bear market Cries for reegulation increase as bruin viewing rapidly gains popularity By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 30, 2001) While the brown bear viewing business in Alaska has been growing for more than a decade, in the last five years it has exploded, leaving land managers and biologists scrambling to figure out how to protect the bears and the sightseers. Consider: On Kodiak Island, after much debate, a new management plan was drafted this past year that puts bear viewers and bear hunters on equal footing when it comes to the island's wildlife trademark. In Southeast Alaska, designated bear viewing areas are operating at capacity, leaving wildlife officials hustling to keep up as tour operators discover and subsequently try to exploit new…

title: PALMERTheft victim faces assault charges for firing at alleged perpetrators
summary: PALMER Theft victim faces assault charges for firing at alleged perpetrators A man who fired a gun at two suspects in a Wasilla vehicle break-in in June has been charged with third-degree assault. The Palmer district attorney's office filed the felony charges last week against Kevin M. Monson, 33. Troopers received a call at 4:40 a.m. June 6 and found Monson holding Hal M. Lee Jr., 21, and Tyler Skeel, 21, both of Eagle River, at gunpoint on the Knik River Bridge on the Glenn Highway about 30 road miles north of Anchorage. Alaska State Troopers said Monson was the victim of a theft from a vehicle, a white Ford van owned by Veco Co. Monson told troopers he spotted Skeel inside the van parked outside Monson's home in Wasilla. Skeel and Lee fled in their van and a chase ensued. According to c…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: SCHOOL LUNCH TODAY Anchorage: Elementary -- Breaded Chicken O's with Tater Tots or cheese pizza or corn dog, mixed fruit, Garfield Bar, milk. Middle -- breaded chicken sandwich with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, bread, Garfield Bar, milk. Mat-Su: Honey Barbecue Dippers, green peas, gelatin with fruit, Tutti Fruitti Quick Bread, milk (white or strawberry). Middle -- Barchetta with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, bread, chocolate chip cookie, milk. Mat-Su: Chicken and cheese tortilla wrap, corn, fresh fruit, M&M chocolate brownie, milk (white or chocolate). TODAY COMMUNITY A…

title: Inside Alaska Business
summary: State Web site earns perfect score The Alaska state government's online posting of public notices and information about elections and legislative actions, and for giving e-mail addresses for its employees earned a perfect score for "digital democracy" in survey results released Tuesday by the Center for Digital Government and the Progress & Freedom Foundation. Lt. Gov. Fran Ulmer, who announced the achievement, is chair of the state's Telecommunications Information Council, which develops policy for state government voice, video and data systems. New Jersey was the only other state earning a perfect score. Chiropractor relocates practice G. Lee Waldroup has relocated his chiropractic practice to the second floor of the Frontier Chiropractic Building at 213 E. Fireweed Lane from Chiropracti…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Community datebook (Published: October 3, 2001) SCHOOL LUNCH TODAY Anchorage: Elementary -- Breaded Chicken O's with Tater Tots or cheese pizza or corn dog, mixed fruit, Garfield Bar, milk. Middle -- breaded chicken sandwich with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, bread, Garfield Bar, milk. Mat-Su: Honey Barbecue Dippers, green peas, gelatin with fruit, Tutti Fruitti Quick Bread, milk (white or strawberry). THURSDAY Anchorage: Elementary -- Teriyaki Wonder Bites with potato wedges or egg roll with Oriental rice or chicken burrito, celery sticks, diced peaches, chocolate chip cookie, milk. Middle -- Barchetta with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, bread, chocolate chip cookie, milk. Mat-Su: Chicken and cheese tortilla wrap, corn, fresh fruit, M&M chocolate…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | City Mortgage faces suits, shuts down LAWSUIT: State accuses chief executive of fraud and mishandling funds. By Wesley Loy And Tony Hopfinger (Published: September 29, 2001) The state is suing Anchorage businessman James Crawford and his embattled City Mortgage Corp., accusing them of mishandling escrow funds and mortgage payments, and defrauding home buyers with a bad vacation offer. Also, on Friday, the state-owned Alaska Housing Finance Corp. and two housing industry companies filed an involuntary bankruptcy petition against City Mortgage. The three say they are owed a combined $48,950, and the petition asks a judge to force City Mortgage into Chapter 11 bankruptcy reorganization. Crawford said Friday he was disappointed by the court actions but that he and the company would coope…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Justice approves new redistricting plan VOTING RIGHTS ACT: Alaska is one of nine states required to be pre-cleared. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: October 4, 2001) Juneau -- The U.S. Department of Justice approved Alaska's 2001 redistricting plan this week, finding that it doesn't dilute the overall political clout of Natives. With the Justice Department's pre-clearance of the plan, it now takes effect while a lawsuit challenging it proceeds. "Alaska's plans have not been pre-cleared previously. It's an important step," said Philip Volland, attorney for the Alaska Redistricting Board. The Justice Department ruled on Monday that the plan complies with the Voting Rights Act of 1965 by preserving the political representation of Alaska Natives. Alaska is one of nine st…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Self-Help (Published: October 4, 2001) Adult Children of Alcoholics, noon Tuesdays and Wednesdays, First Congregational Church, 2610 E. Northern Lights Blvd. Adult Grief Group, noon-1 p.m. first and third Friday, Hospice of Anchorage, 500 West International Airport Road, Suite C. (561-5322) Alaska Amputee Support Group, 7-9 p.m., second Tuesday, Anchorage Unitarian Universalist Fellowship, 3201 Turnagain St. (562-5692, eaglewolf@gci.net ) Alaska Chapter of the National Ovarian Cancer Coalition, 4 p.m. first Sunday, Cancer Therapy Center conference room, Providence Alaska Medical Center. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Health Fairs (Published: October 4, 2001) Health Fair Inc.'s next event is 9 a.m.-1 p.m. Saturday at Four Valleys School in Girdwood. Upcoming fairs include: West Side Fair, 9 a.m.-1 p.m. Oct. 13, Romig Middle School, 2500 Minnesota Drive Chugiak Community Fair, 9 a.m.-noon Oct. 27, Chugiak Senior Center, 22424 N. Birchwood Loop Road (use North Birchwood Loop exit) East Side Fair, 9 a.m.-noon, Nov. 3, Baxter Elementary School, 2991 Baxter Road. Fairs offer free health screenings for blood pressure, height and weight, and vision. Comprehensive blood test for adults 18 and older is $25 (do not eat or drink except water for 12 hours prior to the test); thyroid screening is $35; and prostate disease screening is $30. In all cases, prescription medicine should still be taken, and diabetic…

title: Public debates rail need
summary: Bob Dreyer of Eagle River was like many Americans until the early 1980s. He loved his automobile, swearing he would never bother with public transportation. Then he moved to Melbourne, Australia, finding himself enmeshed in a 48-mile round-trip commute that had him riding trains, buses and street cars to his job. "I hate to say it, but I liked it," Dreyer said Wednesday. He was among a few dozen people who attended an Alaska Railroad hearing Wednesday on a new study that looks at launching commuter rail service between Anchorage and the Mat-Su area. The meeting was at Z.J. Loussac Public Library in Anchorage. Supporters said they've seen commuter rail service work in other cities, contending that it reduces driving stress, highway traffic and pollution. Others believe the market in Anchora…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Teen arrested after high-speed chase on Glenn Highway CHARGES Troopers say youth kicked officer in face after crashing stolen car. By Jeff St. John Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 4, 2001) An Anchorage teenager was arrested early Tuesday morning in Palmer after allegedly forcing an Alaska State Trooper patrol car off the Glenn Highway, leading troopers on a high-speed chase and kicking an officer in the face. Robert Norris, 18, was driving a stolen 1990 Lincoln Continental northbound on the Glenn Highway just north of the Parks Highway intersection at 2:17 a.m., troopers spokesman Greg Wilkinson said. Norris pulled into the southbound lane, forcing Trooper Eric Spitzen to swerve off the road to avoid a collision. Spitzen pulled around and gave chase. Norris drove north past …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | ANWR stays off defense bill CONGRESS: Senate unanimously OKs spending increase. By Carolyn Skorneck The Associated Press (Published: October 4, 2001) Washington -- The Senate passed its $345 billion defense spending bill without a dissenting vote Tuesday after dispensing with Republican objections and a demand that the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge be opened to oil drilling. The vote was 99-0 for the bill that authorizes money for the Defense Department and the military work of the Energy Department for fiscal 2002, which began Monday. The dispute had stymied progress for a week as the nation geared up for war. "The men and women in the military should be able to count on us in normal times, and surely they ought to be able to count on us in these emergency times. And I believe ver…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Center's money diverted SHIP CREEK: Funds for fisheries complex will pay for culverts. By Tim Pryor Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 4, 2001) Former Anchorage Mayor Rick Mystrom's dream of a fisheries complex in Ship Creek has suffered a critical blow. The city made a deal with federal officials to use about $3.2 million once aimed at a salmon research center for an entirely different project: removing some troublesome culverts that crimp the lower reach of the creek and putting in a bridge. Mayor George Wuerch, Mystrom's successor, proposed the change after it became clear that the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration would not go along with the city's plans to build the salmon center, said Wuerch appointee Dick Dworsky on Wednesday. The culverts, installed after …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Mat-Su, Kenai voters hit the polls TODAY: Municipal elections to decide Valley sales tax, Peninsula prison showdown. By S.J. Komarnitsky And Tom Kizzia Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 2, 2001) A proposal to build a private prison in Kenai and a showdown over land use in the Mat-Su Borough are local highlights in today's municipal elections, which are being held in Alaska communities from Ketchikan to Bethel. Mat-Su voters will also take up a proposed 2 percent sales tax. Anchorage residents won't be voting. Municipal elections in Anchorage are held in April. A high-profile referendum on whether the Kenai Peninsula Borough should build Alaska's first private prison has generated an emotional campaign across the Kenai Peninsula. The borough won funding for its plan last spring…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | DA dismisses murder charge By S.J. Komarnitsky Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 2, 2001) Wasilla -- Prosecutors have dismissed a murder charge against a Talkeetna area man whose wife was fatally shot last month. Palmer District Attorney Roman Kalytiak said Monday that he dismissed the charge against Rex Davenport last week because he wanted to collect more evidence before deciding whether to take the case to a grand jury. "I think the issue is going to be, can the state prove murder beyond a reasonable doubt and whether suicide can be excluded by a medical examiner," he said. Alaska State Troopers arrested Davenport, 32, last month and charged him with first-degree murder after he called to report that his wife had been fatally shot at their home just off the Parks Highway ne…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Gottlieb trial begins QUESTION: Kindly healer or drug dealer? By Sheila Toomey Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 2, 2001) There apparently are two Dr. Jeffrey Gottliebs, and an Anchorage jury has been given the job of deciding which one is sitting at the defense table in Judge Elaine Andrews' sixth-floor courtroom. One Dr. Gottlieb is an unpleasant but self-sacrificing healer devoted to easing the pain of patients no other doctors will help. This Dr. Gottlieb has no interest in money. He filled out Medicaid forms incorrectly because it was easier, though he probably could have collected more money if he filled them out right. Actually, Medicaid called him and asked him to take on some difficult patients other doctors refused to see. That was the Dr. Gottlieb presented to the j…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Business People (Published: October 1, 2001) The University of Alaska Small Business Development Center announced Steven Tollett will be its business counselor, providing free and confidential counseling to new and existing businesses. Tollett's experience with small business ownership includes owning a seasonal charter fishing business, the center said. Mary Grisco was hired as executive director for the All Alaska Pediatric Partnership. Grisco brings 20 years of nonprofit administration experience to the partnership, a health coalition to foster collaborative programs that improve the health of Alaska's children. Dr. Robert Thompson joined the practice of Dr. Sandra Denton at the Alaska Alternative Medicine Clinic. Thompson is board certified in obstetrics and gynecology, with spec…
